title: The Deal That Isn't — And the Power Vacuum It Creates
subhead: The US-Iran peace framework is stalling on the very terms — Hormuz and nukes — that make Turkey indispensable. Every day the deal doesn't close, Turkey's leverage as mediator, energy corridor, and defence supplier compounds. Every day it does close without Turkey's institutional fingerprints, that leverage evaporates.

sections.heading: The 14-Point Mirage: Iran Questions, China Mediates, Turkey Watches
sections.body:
Iran confirmed it is evaluating a US 14-point peace proposal, but described parts of the framework as 'unrealistic,' according to Iran's foreign ministry statement to CNBC. Pakistan's government claims a breakthrough could come within 24-72 hours, but Tehran has pushed back on key provisions — particularly those concerning its nuclear program and the terms for reopening the Strait of Hormuz. Meanwhile, Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi met Chinese counterpart Wang Yi in Beijing, with Wang calling for Hormuz to reopen 'as soon as possible' and Araghchi stressing Iran wants a 'fair and comprehensive agreement.' The Araqchi-Wang meeting comes one week before the Trump-Xi summit in Beijing. Trump paused the Operation Freedom Hormuz escort mission, citing 'progress toward a deal,' while simultaneously threatening 'higher-level strikes' if Iran rejects terms. Rubio declared the War Powers Act '100 percent unconstitutional,' staking out maximal executive authority over the conflict.

The structural reality: the deal's two hardest terms — Iran's nuclear future and Hormuz control — are precisely the issues where Turkey's geographic and diplomatic position gives it maximum leverage, and precisely the issues where China is now inserting itself as the alternative channel. Araghchi's Beijing visit, not Ankara, signals that Tehran is building a negotiation track through China that bypasses the Pakistan-Turkey-Egypt-Saudi quartet. If the final agreement is brokered through Beijing rather than Islamabad, the quartet's institutional claim on the post-war order evaporates overnight.

Trump's pause of Operation Freedom is tactical, not strategic — a pressure valve, not a retreat. Oil prices dropped to $97/bbl on deal-progress headlines (BBC) but remain at war-elevated levels. The Hormuz blockade persists. Iraq is offering 25%+ discounts for tankers braving the strait (Bloomberg, Telegraph). The commercial reality hasn't changed; only the diplomatic signaling has.

The decisive move for Turkey is not to compete with China for mediation primacy — that contest is already being lost. It is to position itself as the indispensable implementer of whatever deal emerges: Hormuz demining, post-conflict security guarantees, and energy corridor infrastructure. Foreign Minister Fidan already signaled on April 25 that Turkey would consider a role in Hormuz demining after a deal. That offer needs to become a demand for institutionalized participation in the implementation mechanism, not a voluntary contribution after the fact.
sections.whyItMatters: Mediation leverage is perishable — it exists only while the conflict is active and no alternative channel is functioning. China's diplomatic surge into the Iran file threatens to displace the quartet just as the quartet was consolidating. If the deal closes through Beijing, Turkey gets a thank-you note, not a seat at the post-war table.

sections.heading: SAHA 2026: The Defence Export Machine Hits Scale — But Can It Sustain?
sections.body:
Turkey's defence and aviation exports surged 28% in the first four months of 2026, reaching $2.8 billion, Trade Minister Omer Bolat announced at the SAHA 2026 expo in Istanbul. Last year the sector crossed $10 billion for the first time. The sector now encompasses 3,500 companies and nearly 100,000 workers, with a project backlog worth $100 billion and $20 billion in national income contribution. At SAHA, Bolat projected over $10 billion in contracts would be signed during the fair. Turkey exported $3.7 billion in rockets, missiles, and smart munitions last year, and $2.1 billion in unmanned aerial vehicles.

Turkey's defence industry has crossed the threshold from national sufficiency to export-driven strategic influence. The $10 billion export figure is no longer aspirational — it's the revenue base that funds R&D, sustains production lines, and gives Ankara leverage over the military postures of importing states. Canada's Minister of State for Defence Procurement Stephen Fuhr visited SAHA with a full delegation to discuss increasing cooperation. Türkiye-Kenya signed a defence cooperation agreement at the expo. Turkey is no longer selling drones to individual buyers — it is building defence ecosystems with client states across Africa and the Gulf.

The Pasifik Teknoloji deal to export 100,000 FPV kamikaze drones, reported by Daily Sabah on May 5, illustrates the scale. This isn't boutique aerospace — it's industrial-scale loitering munition production at volumes that reshape the tactical options of importing militaries. Baykar debuted the Mızrak smart loitering munition at SAHA. Aselsan presented five new products and multiple upgrades.

The trap: export volumes without platform integration are fragile. A state buys 100,000 kamikaze drones; it also needs training, maintenance, C4ISR integration, and replenishment contracts. Turkey's competitive advantage right now is price and availability — not the deep systems integration that lock in US or European suppliers. The decisive move is building the service-and-sustainment ecosystem around every export deal, converting one-time sales into long-term defence dependencies. Without that, the next cheaper supplier displaces Turkey the way Turkey displaced others.
sections.whyItMatters: Defence exports are Turkey's fastest pathway to strategic influence — every purchasing state becomes a stakeholder in Turkey's industrial continuity. But exports without integration are transactional, not structural. The $100 billion project backlog is a promise; the delivery chain is the proof.

sections.heading: Inflation at 32.37%: The CBRT Holds, Energy Bites
sections.body:
Turkey's annual inflation hit 32.37% in April, the highest since October 2025, with monthly inflation at 4.18% — well above the 3.28% forecast. The drivers are structural, not cyclical: housing inflation at 7.99% monthly, transport at 4.29%, clothing at 8.94%. Treasury Minister Şimşek called the rise 'temporary,' but the data tells a different story. The Central Bank kept its benchmark rate steady at its last meeting, explicitly citing Iran war fallout and 'potential second-round effects.' The CBRT's year-end forecast range of 15%-21% is now structurally unreachable — even the government's own disinflation trajectory requires energy prices to normalize, which requires Hormuz to reopen, which requires a deal that Iran is currently questioning.

Turkey's inflation is a supply-shock phenomenon wearing a demand-management mask. The CBRT cannot interest-rate its way out of a strait blockade. Every $1 rise in oil prices adds roughly $400 million to Turkey's annual energy bill. Brent at $97 means Turkey's energy import bill is running hundreds of millions above the budget baseline that Şimşek's disinflation projections assumed.

The government has deployed a fuel pricing mechanism and agricultural cost shields, but these are fiscal patches, not structural corrections. The domestic producer price index rose 3.17% monthly — ING's analysis flags refined petroleum, chemicals, and natural gas as the primary PPI drivers, all tied to the Hormuz disruption. S&P Global sharply hiked its Turkish inflation outlook over war-related energy costs in March. The CBRT presents its second inflation report next week; analysts expect both target and forecast revisions upward.

The decisive move isn't monetary — it's infrastructural. The Hormuz crisis has made Turkey's case for becoming an alternative energy transit corridor more compelling than any policy paper could. The Eastern Mediterranean pipeline, the Qatar-Turkey gas pipeline through post-Assad Syria, and Caspian corridor routes are all now structurally viable. The trap is waiting for Hormuz to normalize and missing the window to lock in alternative transit infrastructure while the crisis makes the case.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey's energy import dependency is not a variable — it's a constant. The only variable is whether the next supply shock finds Turkey still dependent on a single chokepoint or diversified through its own territory. Every month of elevated inflation is a policy subsidy for building that infrastructure; every month of inaction is a tax on the future.

sections.heading: Italy-Turkey Defence Axis: The Bilateral Bypass Around EU Exclusion
sections.body:
Italy and Turkey are building a defence partnership that operates below the EU institutional ceiling — and increasingly renders that ceiling irrelevant to their mutual security interests. The Italian Navy confirmed in March 2026 that the Baykar TB3 UCAV could be integrated aboard the aircraft carrier ITS Cavour, with a formal Italian acquisition projected for Q3 2026, making Italy the first European TB3 operator. Baykar CEO Haluk Bayraktar confirmed the timeline. Italy's Army Chief of Staff General Carmine Masiello visited Ankara for bilateral defence talks. Turkey is negotiating to co-produce the Franco-Italian SAMP/T air and missile defence system — with Italy supporting the trilateral cooperation while France has historically blocked progress over political objections. The partnership extends to the T625 Gökbey helicopter program (with Spain) and Turkey's planned purchase of 40 Eurofighter Typhoons from the consortium including Italy's Leonardo.

The Rome-Ankara axis is the working model for how Turkey bypasses PESCO exclusion without confronting it: bilateral defence industrial integration that creates facts on the ground faster than EU bureaucracy can process them. France blocks SAMP/T co-production at the EU level; Italy enables it bilaterally. The EU excludes Turkey from PESCO; Italy integrates Turkey into its carrier aviation, helicopter manufacturing, and missile defence supply chain. The pattern is consistent: where institutional doors close, bilateral windows open — and Italy is holding them.

The context is urgent: NATO has intercepted four Iranian missiles heading toward Turkey since the Iran war began, exposing gaps in long-range air defence on the alliance's southeastern flank. SAMP/T integration would become a central pillar of Turkey's Steel Dome. The July 7-8 NATO summit in Ankara — the first time Turkey hosts allied leaders since 2004 — gives Rome and Ankara a shared platform to consolidate this partnership within the alliance framework.

The trap: bilateral bypass works until it doesn't. If France decides to escalate its SAMP/T obstruction from political delay to contractual veto within the Eurosam consortium, Italy's goodwill cannot override French co-ownership rights. The decisive move is to convert bilateral goodwill into multilateral commitment at the Ankara NATO summit — locking Italy's support into alliance frameworks that France cannot unilaterally block.
sections.whyItMatters: PESCO exclusion is a structural amputation, but Italy is proving it's not a fatal one — if Turkey can institutionalize bilateral defence ties faster than EU bureaucracy can exclude them. The Rome-Ankara axis is either the model for Turkey's European defence integration or a temporary workaround that collapses when French obstruction hardens.

sections.heading: The Post-European Security Moment: Turkey Outgrows the Frame
sections.body:
Daily Sabah's analysis frames Turkey's current position as a 'post-European security moment' — not leaving European security, but operating beyond the frame Europe provides. The argument is structural: the EU has frozen accession, built a defence architecture that marginalizes Ankara, and allowed bilateral vetoes to harden. Meanwhile, Turkey has developed geography, military capacity, diplomatic access, and defence-industrial instruments to shape security outcomes across multiple theatres simultaneously. The European Political Community summit in Yerevan on May 5 produced statements on Ukraine, energy, and US indifference; Turkey's quartet produced a ceasefire. The contrast is the argument.

Europe's voice in the regional security conversation is present but not formative. The quartet — Turkey, Saudi Arabia, Egypt, Pakistan — constitutes the most operationally significant diplomatic formation in the post-Iran war order, and Europe is not in it. In the Black Sea, Turkey's Montreux Convention authority makes it the indispensable actor in every naval access calculation. In Syria, Turkey holds a military agreement with Damascus, a multi-billion-dollar trade relationship, and a direct stake in stabilization that no European government can match. In Africa, France's security architecture has collapsed across the Sahel, and Turkey is filling the vacuum with defence cooperation agreements like the Kenya deal signed at SAHA 2026.

The Economist reported that Ukraine's Zelensky explicitly linked the risk of US NATO withdrawal to the need for a European security architecture that includes Turkey — a recognition that Europe's defence calculus cannot exclude Turkey without becoming incoherent. NATO chief Rutte says Europe has 'gotten Trump's message on defence,' but getting the message and having the capacity are different things. The US is withdrawing 5,000 troops from Germany. European defence spending pledges of $350 billion by 2035, per Reuters Breakingviews, would need to increase further if the US exits NATO entirely.

The trap for Turkey: indispensability that isn't institutionalized is indispensability that expires. The 'Europe needs us more than we need Europe' framing, which Erdoğan has stated publicly, is half-true — and the wrong half to emphasize. The decisive move is converting operational indispensability into institutional access: offering Europe concrete security value — Steel Dome integration, Black Sea air defence, migration containment, energy corridor redundancy — in exchange for PESCO participation, defense procurement access, and a formalized role in the post-war European security architecture. Rhetorical indispensability produces resentment; contractual indispensability produces architecture.
sections.whyItMatters: The post-European security order is being built in real time, and Turkey is shaping outcomes that European institutions cannot reach. But outgrowing a frame without building a replacement is exposure, not autonomy. Turkey needs institutional architecture as much as it needs ICBMs and Steel Dome — and the window to build it is open exactly as long as the current crisis makes Turkey indispensable.

sections.heading: Eastern Mediterranean Energy: The Pipeline That the War Makes Possible
sections.body:
The Iran-Hormuz crisis has revived two pipeline projects previously considered politically impossible: the EastMed pipeline connecting Eastern Mediterranean gas to Europe, and the Qatar-Turkey gas pipeline through post-Assad Syria. The EastMed Institute analysis, published April 28, notes that the 2026 crisis makes both routes structurally viable for the first time. The fall of Assad removed the primary political obstacle to a Qatar-Turkey pipeline crossing Syrian territory. The Hormuz blockade makes every alternative route to European markets commercially competitive at current energy prices.

Every day Hormuz remains closed is a subsidy for alternative transit infrastructure through Turkey. The strategic logic has shifted from 'these pipelines are desirable' to 'these pipelines are necessary' — and the political conditions for both have converged for the first time in a decade.

The Eastern Mediterranean gas reserves — Israel's Leviathan, Cyprus's Aphrodite, Egypt's Zohr — all need routes to European markets that don't transit Hormuz. Turkey's geography makes it the natural land bridge. The EU's own energy diversification imperative, driven by the Russia-Ukraine war and now compounded by the Hormuz disruption, aligns with Turkish infrastructure ambitions. European gas prices dipped on news of Hormuz reopening efforts (Reuters), but the structural incentive for alternative routes persists regardless of whether the strait reopens — because the next closure is always possible.

The decisive move is to move from advocacy to construction. Turkey needs to accelerate the regulatory, commercial, and security frameworks for both pipelines while the crisis makes them politically and commercially compelling. The trap is treating the current energy shock as temporary and reverting to pre-war procurement patterns once prices normalize — because the next shock will find Turkey equally dependent, equally exposed, and equally unable to negotiate from strength.
sections.whyItMatters: Pipeline infrastructure takes years to build and decades to repay. The window of political and commercial viability is open now — driven by the convergence of the Assad collapse, the Hormuz crisis, and European energy desperation. That window won't stay open forever. Turkey is either the energy corridor for the next generation of European supply, or it's a bystander watching other routes get built.

bottomLine: The US-Iran peace framework is stalling on the two issues — nuclear and Hormuz — that give Turkey maximum leverage, while China inserts itself as the alternative mediation channel. That convergence defines the structural risk: Turkey's indispensability as mediator, energy corridor, and defence supplier is compounding daily, but none of it is institutionalized. The quartet brokered a ceasefire; Beijing may broker the deal. The SAHA expo showcased $10 billion in export capacity; the export ecosystem lacks the integration depth that makes clients dependent. The CBRT holds rates while energy-driven inflation runs 32.37%; the only structural fix is transit infrastructure, not monetary policy. Italy is proving bilateral defence integration can bypass EU exclusion; France is proving bilateral goodwill can't override contractual vetoes. The Eastern Mediterranean pipeline projects that were politically impossible a year ago are commercially necessary now — but advocacy is not construction. Every one of these threads converges on a single structural reality: Turkey has built the instruments of strategic autonomy faster than it has built the institutional architecture to sustain them. The crisis makes Turkey indispensable; the post-crisis order will determine whether that indispensability was converted into permanent power or squandered as temporary leverage. The clock is not running out — it is already past midnight on several of these decisions.
sources: Reuters, AP News, BBC, CNBC, Daily Sabah, Anadolu Agency, Al Jazeera, Moneycontrol, Stimson Center, SCMP, Bloomberg, The Telegraph, ING, S&P Global, The Economist, EastMed Institute/EISMENA, Defense News, Al-Monitor, Times of Israel, NATO.int, MSN/COP31, FRPI, Canadian Defence Procurement (via Anadolu Agency)
